coredata.dc:title: Nanocatalytic and antimicrobial potency of CQD@Pd nanoparticles: Experimental and theoretical approach
coredata.dc:creator: Vijeata, Anjali
Garg, Ankita
Ram Chaudhary, Ganga
Chaudhary, Savita
Bhalla, Aman
coredata.dc:description: A single-pot approach has been developed for the fabrication of novel heterogeneous nanocatalyst of carbon quantum dots doped palladium (CQD@Pd-NPs) nanoparticles. Herein, Aegle marmelos fruit extract was utilized as a reducing and capping agent for the fabrication of CQD@Pd-NPs. Synthesis of metallic nanoparticles through green chemistry route is environment beneficial technology owing to its low cost and ecological compatibility. The formation of CQD-NPs and CQD@Pd-NPs was confirmed with high-end sophisticated techniques and theoretical studies. The computational analysis revealed a discernible decrease in the band gap value (E gap = E LUMO - E HOMO ) from 0.08 eV to 0.03 eV upon the introduction of Pd atoms into carbon quantum dots (CQDs). Therefore, computational data also authenticates the stability of the CQD@Pd-NPs nanocatalyst which was predominantly supported by electrostatic interaction between Pd and CQDs. The developed nanocatalyst has vouched for an effective candidature in the Suzuki-Miyaura and Mizoroki-Heck coupling reactions with a superior selectivity for product formation. The excellent antibacterial properties against E. coli and S. aureus were further supported by visualizing bacterial growth, inhibition zone, FESEM results and molecular docking studies. Moreover, the nanocatalyst displayed facile separation from the reaction system and showcased efficient recyclability with minimal activity loss. This further supports an effective potential over existing commercial catalysts and holds immense future prospective in industrial applications.

head.ce:title: Nanocatalytic and antimicrobial potency of CQD@Pd nanoparticles: Experimental and theoretical approach

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry and Centre of Advanced Studies in Chemistry, Panjab University, Chandigarh 160014, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry and Centre of Advanced Studies in Chemistry

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry and Centre of Advanced Studies in Chemistry, Panjab University, Chandigarh 160014, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A single-pot approach has been developed for the fabrication of novel heterogeneous nanocatalyst of carbon quantum dots doped palladium (CQD@Pd-NPs) nanoparticles. Herein, Aegle marmelos fruit extract was utilized as a reducing and capping agent for the fabrication of CQD@Pd-NPs. Synthesis of metallic nanoparticles through green chemistry route is environment beneficial technology owing to its low cost and ecological compatibility. The formation of CQD-NPs and CQD@Pd-NPs was confirmed with high-end sophisticated techniques and theoretical studies. The computational analysis revealed a discernible decrease in the band gap value (E gap = E LUMO - E HOMO ) from 0.08 eV to 0.03 eV upon the introduction of Pd atoms into carbon quantum dots (CQDs). Therefore, computational data also authenticates the stability of the CQD@Pd-NPs nanocatalyst which was predominantly supported by electrostatic interaction between Pd and CQDs. The developed nanocatalyst has vouched for an effective candidature in the Suzuki-Miyaura and Mizoroki-Heck coupling reactions with a superior selectivity for product formation. The excellent antibacterial properties against E. coli and S. aureus were further supported by visualizing bacterial growth, inhibition zone, FESEM results and molecular docking studies. Moreover, the nanocatalyst displayed facile separation from the reaction system and showcased efficient recyclability with minimal activity loss. This further supports an effective potential over existing commercial catalysts and holds immense future prospective in industrial applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The escalated growth in the chemical, material, and pharmaceutical sectors has explored a diverse range of metal-based catalysts. Large varieties of organic transformations (including hydrogenation, oxidation, Stille coupling, Suzuki reaction, and several cross-coupling reactions) are highly supported by the catalytic activities of metal-based catalysts [1–3]. However, the competence of metal-based catalysts particularly in the nano-metric dimensions has been enhanced to several extents because of their distinctive surface properties. Amongst a diverse range of transition metals-based nanocatalyst, palladium nanoparticles (Pd-NPs) based catalysts hold significant consideration among researchers in the field of heterogeneous catalysis. Pd-supported catalyst is one of the effective alternatives for electro-oxidation reactions [4–6]. The superior electrical conductivity of Pd-NPs has further supported its application in gas sensing [7,8]. The high stability in alkaline media and effective confrontation against leaching or de-activation during catalytic activities in Pd-NPs has further assured their durable performances and reusability in the reaction system. The comparatively profuse and reasonably priced nature of Pd-NPs formulates them as substantial materials for industrial applications [9,10]. However, the low aqueous solubility and agglomeration tendency of Pd-NPs have restricted their efficiency in catalytic reactions. Moreover, the homogenous types of Pd nanocatalysts are difficult to recycle from reaction media and compel us to promote superior Pd-based heterogeneous nano-catalysts.

Until now, a diverse range of methodologies has been explored to develop highly dispersed Pd-based heterogeneous nano-catalysts. For instance, the fabrication of Pd-NPs via using chemical reduction method in the presence of external stabilizing agents such as glycols, surfactants, or biopolymers are widely employed to prevent the aggregation rate and enhance the dispersion ability of particles. However, the use of external stabilizers has reduced the catalytic efficiency of Pd-NPs and restricted their large-scale production. Thus, the usage of supporting materials including MOFs, metal oxides, and carbon-based materials such as carbon quantum dots can boost the catalytic activity of Pd-NPs and holds significant importance in the industrial and academic sectors [11–13]. In the most recent decade, the use of biological materials as reducing agents has copiously gained much attention as it reduces the punitive effects and enhances the catalytic performance of metal-based nanomaterials. Moreover, the biosynthesis of the metallic nanoparticles benefits from the use of hazardous chemicals and provides a cheaper and greener approach towards the development of suitable nanocatalyst. The concept of green chemistry thus becomes more novel and dependable strategy for the synthesis of metallic nanoparticles. Out of all valuable supports, the utilization of carbon-based nanoparticles especially, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are one of the most significant and affordable materials to enhance the efficiency of Pd-NPs in catalysis. Additionally, the ease in preparation and utilization of waste biomass materials for the synthesis of CQDs has further made the choice of support material for generating heterogeneous catalysts of Pd-NPs. A single-step bio reduction method using plant extracts could thus be an economic pathway to deal with fabrication of metallic nanoparticles.The ecofriendly nature, high conductivity and available surface-active states with enhanced photoluminescence activities of CQDs further boost the electro-catalytic efficiency of Pd-NPs. Based on these merits; CQDs-supported Pd-NPs have displayed a symbolic role in organic transformations, pharmaceuticals, and environmental remediation applications [14].

Herein, we report a convenient and effective single-pot approach for the synthesis of heterogeneous nanocatalysts of carbon quantum dots doped palladium (CQD@Pd-NPs) nanoparticles. The fabrication of CQDs was done from the outer peel of Aegle marmelos fruit. Aegle marmelos belonging to the Rutaceae family is one of the widely distributed native trees of the Indian subcontinent and Southeast Asia [15–17]. The fruit is enriched in Xanthotoxol, tannins, alkaloids, and flavonoids along with a significant amount of phosphorous, potassium, calcium, magnesium, and iron, etc [18]. The fruit plays a binary role of reducer and capping agent for clustering and functionalizing the Pd-NPs. Particularly, the bioactive molecules present in the fruit such as flavonoids, polysaccharides, and tannins have the ability to reduce Pd2+ ions and efficiently functionalize the formed nanocatalyst with distinct functional groups [19]. The presence of these bioactive compounds also stimulates the anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial potential of the nanomaterials. Furthermore, the computational analysis revealed a discernible decrease in the band gap value (E gap = E LUMO – E HOMO ) from 0.08 eV to 0.03 eV upon the introduction of Pd atoms into carbon quantum dots (CQDs). The formation of CQD@Pd-NPs was comprehensively verified with high-end sophisticated techniques and theoretical studies. The developed nanocatalyst has vouched for an effective candidature for producing the corresponding products with favourable yields in Suzuki-Miyaura and Mizoroki-Heck coupling reactions. A diverse range of Suzuki-Miyaura and Mizoroki-Heck coupled products were successfully isolated by utilizing moderate reaction conditions, reduced reaction time, and the use of water as an environmentally friendly medium without the need of any further reagents by using CQD@Pd-NPs. The excellent antibacterial properties of the developed nanocatalyst against E. coli and S. aureus were further checked by optimizing the inhibition zone, detailed bacterial growth, and FESEM studies. The facile separation from the reaction system with efficient recyclability supports the effective potential of CQD@Pd-NPs over existing commercial catalysts and embraces a colossal range of potential applications in the industrial sector.

Palladium (II) chloride, Dimethylformamide (DMF), toluene, absolute ethanol, potassium carbonate (K2CO3), sodium carbonate (Na2CO3), caesium carbonate (Cs2CO3), triethylamine (NEt3), sodium hydroxide (NaOH), and substituted phenyl boronic acid (PhB (OH)2), was purchased from Sigma Aldrich with 99 % purity. Prior to the usage, DMF and toluene were dried using matrix molecular sieves of 5 Å with a mesh size of 30–40. Nutrient broth, Nutrient agar, and Mannitol salt agar base were procured from Himedia. The acquired compounds consisting of substituted styrene and aryl halide derivatives were purchased from Avra Synthesis Pvt. Ltd. Column chromatography was accomplished using Merck Silica Gel (60–120 mesh as the stationary phase), while elution was carried out utilizing mixtures of ethyl acetate (EtOAc) and n-hexane. An analytical thin-layer chromatography (TLC) was executed on pre-coated silica gel F254 aluminum plates, followed by visualization under UV light. All the reagents and solvents were utilized in their original form without any further purification. Ultrapure water, 18.2 MΩ cm−1 was obtained from the cascada bio water filtration unit (Pall Corporation Ann Arbour, Michigan, USA).

The exocarp of Aegle marmelos (Bael patra) fruit was used to obtain the extract. The formation process involves the thorough washing of fresh fruit of Aegle marmelos with water followed by fine chopping of the outer layer (exocarp) into small pieces. Consequently, the chopped pieces of exocarp were placed in a silica crucible and dried in the oven at 100 °C for 2 h to remove its moisture content (Fig. 1 a). The obtained residue was homogeneously ground into a fine powder with the help of an electric grinder. Particularly, 10 g of the resulting powder was then subjected to thermal calcination in a muffle furnace at 200 °C for 90 min. The obtained brown-coloured ash was again ground with a mortar pestle. Around 1 g of ground ash was then mixed in 500 mL of water. The suspension was then stirred for 12 h at 500 rpm for complete dispersion. The as prepared solution was then centrifuged at 7000 rpm for 20 min and dried overnight at 100 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of heterogeneous nanocatalyst of CQD@Pd-NPs
ce:section.ce:para: Briefly, a 50 mL solution of Aegle marmelos extract (1 mg/mL) was mixed with a 50 mL solution of palladium chloride (0.01 M). The mixture was heated under reflux conditions at 50 °C for 12 h under stirring at 750 rpm. The formation of nanoparticles was examined by a change in the color of the solution from yellowish to dark brownish, which assures the complete reduction of Pd (II) to Pd (0). The obtained suspension was then centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 10 min to obtain CQD@Pd-NPs. The supernatant left with an excess of amount of plant extract was discarded and the obtained pellet was 2–3 times washed with water and ethanol. The formed solution was then dried in the oven at 70 °C overnight (Fig. 1a) and used for further detailed characterization.

The UV–vis. absorption spectrum was examined through Jasco UV–visible (UV–vis.) spectrophotometer using a quartz cuvette of 1 cm path length and working range between 200 and 800 nm. The surface nature of CQD@Pd-NPs was obtained using the Perkin Elmer (RXI) FTIR spectrometer instrument (400 cm−1 to 4000 cm−1). The heating and stirring process was carried out on the IKA C-MAG HS7 instrument. The ultracentrifugation was performed on the REMI R-24 centrifuge. The power sonic-404 sonicator was used for the dissolution process. Wide angle (2θ = 5–90°) XRD (X-Ray powder diffraction) patterns of CQD@Pd-NPs were obtained on Panalytical X’Pert Pro XRD diffractometer using monochromatic CuKαradiation(λ = 1.5418 Å) and scan rate of 2 deg.min−1. The morphology and elemental analysis were carried out on field emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM) instrument (Hitachi SU8010 microscope). The size of the nanocatalyst was further determined via high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM Hitachi H-7500 electron microscope) at 100 KV. The thermal properties of the sample were investigated by thermo-gravimetric analysis (TGA-DTA) on the SDT Q-600 instrument. DSC (Differential scanning calorimeter) measurements were performed on the METTLER TOLEDO (Q20) instrument. 1H (500 MHz) and 13C (125 MHz) NMR spectra were recorded using BRUKER advanced-II spectrometers. Perfit GSI-MP-3 instrument was used to measure the melting of the sample in open capillaries.
The catalytic activities of CQD@Pd-NPs have been investigated by carrying out the Suzuki-Miyaura and Mizoroki-Heck coupling reactions as follows:

In a typical experiment, a 25 mL round bottom flask was filled with a mixture consisting of 1.0 mmol of aryl halides, 1.1 mmol of aryl boronic acid (phenyl boronic acid), 2 mmol of the base, 2 mg of CQD@Pd-NPs nanocatalyst, and 5 mL of 1:1 mixture of ethanol and water. The reaction mixture flask was subjected to stirring at room temperature for 20 min and the completion of the reaction was assessed via thin-layer chromatography. Upon the completion of the chemical transformation, the catalyst was effectively isolated from the reaction mixture through the process of centrifugation which was subsequently accompanied by the dilution of the residual solution with water. The desired products were extracted from the aqueous phase using ethyl acetate and dried using anhydrous sodium sulphate. The re-crystallization of the products was done using ethanol and further purified by column-chromatography using ethyl acetate: hexane as eluant. The 1H and 13C NMR data of the resulting products were taken (please refer to Supplementary material) and compared with existing literature [20–22].

For Mizoroki-Heck coupling reaction, a solution comprising 1.0 mmol of aryl halides, 1.5 mmol of styrene, 2 mmol of base, 5 mg of CQD@Pd-NPs nanocatalyst, and 5 mL of a mixture of ethanol and water (1:1) was subjected to heating at 80 °C for 30 mins, and the reaction was monitored by TLC. The catalyst was effectively isolated from the reaction mixture through the process of centrifugation and subsequently, the reaction mixture was subjected to a cooling process until it reached room temperature. Afterwards, the resulting mixture was treated with ethyl acetate for extraction from the aqueous phase and was desiccated using anhydrous sodium sulphate. Afterward, the solvent was evaporated under reduced pressure to yield the desired products and re-crystallization was done in ethanol. All the synthesized derivatives were purified via column chromatography employing ethyl acetate and n-hexane as a solvent system. The structural characterization was done using 1H and 13C NMR (please refer to Supplementary material) and data was compared with the reported literature [23–25].

The antimicrobial activities of CQD@Pd-NPs were examined as a function of different concentrations of nanocatalyst by employing agar well diffusion assay against E. coli and S. aureus. For the analysis, both the chosen bacterial strains were incubated in nutrient broth and left overnight at 37 ± 1 °C for 12 h [26–28]. For the susceptibility test, 50 μL/mL of E. coli and S. aureus were spread on nutrient agar and mannitol agar plates respectively. Subsequently, 8 mm wells were bored on agar plates by stainless steel cork borer followed by the addition of 100 μL of each concentration. Distilled water served as a control during the experiment. The plates were then incubated at 37 °C in an incubator for 24 h followed by the measurement of inhibition zones. The experiment was performed in triplicates and the average values were plotted.

The morphological and topographical changes in bacterial strain were further measured via FESEM. For the analysis, both bacterial strains were treated with CQD@Pd-NPs and incubated for 3 h at 37 °C. After incubation, the samples were harvested via centrifugation at 3500 rpm for 10 min and were subsequently washed thrice with PBS buffer solution. The obtained cells were re-suspended in 2.5 % glutaraldehyde solution and frozen at −4 °C for 1 h. The fixed bacterial cells were dehydrated with different ethanol solutions ranging from 50 %, 60 %, 70 %, 80 %, 90 % and 100 % via centrifugation at 4000 rpm [29,30]. The treated and untreated bacterial strains were retrieved from the respective samples and subjected to FESEM analysis.

The complexation of CQDs with Pd-NPs was further assessed by theoretical studies carried out by using Gaussian 09 software. Firstly, CQDs were subjected to initial optimization, alongside their counterparts of Pd2+ ions. The B3LYP functional was employed to evaluate all relevant computational calculations. The 6-311G* basis set for CQDs and the LANL2DZ basis set for the substantial palladium (Pd) atoms were used for the studies. The highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) were also generated with an iso-value- of 0.02.

The use of molecular docking studies to elucidate the underlying mechanism of a given bioactivity of Pd-NPS and CQDs has garnered significant interest. Herein, we conducted molecular docking investigations of CQDs and Pd-NPs with specific enzyme targets, namely β-lactamase and DNA gyrase derived from E. coli and S. aureus. As β-lactamase and DNA gyrase, both have played a crucial role in bacterial viability. Thus, these enzymes were chosen as potential targets for this study. The X-ray crystal structures of β-lactamase E. coli (PDB ID- 4KZ6), DNA gyrase E.coli (PDB ID- 7P2X), β-lactamase S.aureus (PDB ID- 1MWU) and DNA gyrase S.aureus (PDB ID- 5Z4O) were retrieved from the Protein Data Bank. Docking studies were conducted by employing Auto dockTools1.5.6. software. For the analysis, proteins are geared up by eliminating all water molecules, followed by the introduction of polar hydrogen and Kollman charges. The monomer unit structure of CQDs and their complex with Pd-NPs were taken from the DFT studies after optimization studies on their energy minimization using Gaussian 09 software. This process led to the construction of a stable geometry of CQDs, which was then confirmed by docking analysis. The Lamarckian genetic algorithm (LGA) was employed as a prominent search algorithm with 100 autonomous iterations. Discovery Studio was exploited to visualize the outcomes of molecular docking, specifically binding interactions between selected enzymes and nanocatalysts as well as their three-dimensional visualization.

A single-pot approach has been developed for the fabrication of novel heterogeneous nanocatalysts of carbon quantum dots doped palladium (CQD@Pd-NPs) nanoparticles using the exocarp of Aegle Marmelos fruit (Fig. 1a). The existing flavonoids, terpenoids and other polyphenolic compounds in Aegle Marmelos derived CQDs act as a reducing agent for converting Pd (II) to Pd (0). The detailed characterization of bare CQDs has been explained in supporting information (Section S.1). The mode of interactions among CQDs and Pd-Nps were further verified using the theoretical calculations of density functional theory (DFT). The formation of CQD@Pd-NPs was tested by the comparison of the HOMO and LUMO energy levels of formed CQDs and their subsequent interaction with palladium (Pd) atoms. These calculations and analyses about the energies of molecular orbitals help unravel the formation process of CQD@Pd-NPs. Initially, the CQDs and their doping with palladium (Pd) atoms were subjected to geometric optimization for their molecular orbital energies (Fig. 1b). The electronic characteristics, such as the energies of the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO), the band gap, and the dipole moment were also calculated for the better understanding of the interaction mechanism between CQDs and Pd-NPs. The results of the calculations showed that there was an observable reduction in the band gap value (E gap = E LUMO - E HOMO ) from 0.08 eV to 0.03 eV by adding Pd atoms in CQDs. In addition, there is a significant increase in the dipole moment from 7.912 Debye to 9.246 Debye. These theoretical calculations support the successful interaction between CQDs and palladium atoms and further confirm the molecular stability of CQD@Pd-NPs (Fig. 1b). The UV–vis. absorption spectra of CQD@Pd-NPs provide a sharp excitonic peak at 221 nm and a broad peak at 338 nm. The shift in the peak position of PdCl2 from 425 nm to 338 nm indicates the distortion of Pd-Cl bond and linkage of Pd with the aromatic ring of quantum dots (Fig. 1c). Additionally, the absorption peak at 221 nm could be attributed to the combinational effect of n-π* and π-π* transition of heterogenous atoms, linked with the double bonds in the aromatic ring complex [31,32]. While the less intense absorption band at 338 nm indicates the complete reduction of Pd (II) to Pd (0), thereby indicating the fabrication of CQD@Pd-NPs (Fig. 1c). The dark brownish coloration further supports the complete reduction of PdCl2 salt and successful formation of CQD@Pd-NPs. The FTIR spectrum of CQD@Pd-NPs showed a sharp –OH stretching peak at 3420 cm−1. The weak band at 2064 cm−1 and sharp absorption peak at 1620 cm−1 demonstrate the existence of CN and CO functional groups over the surface of the CQD@Pd-NPs nanocatalyst (Fig. 1d). The weak band at 1394 cm−1 could be assigned to the N–H stretching vibration or the bending vibration of OH bond associated with the polysaccharides present in the CQDs [33]. Additionally, the existence of sharp peaks at 1139 and 621 cm−1 represents the CO and PdO stretching vibrations in CQD@Pd-NPs respectively (Fig. 1d). The existence of distinct functional groups i.e., CN, CO, OH, CO, and PdO supports the successful linkage of Pd ions with the graphitic structure of CQDs, resulting in the formation of the stable complex of CQD@Pd-NPs. EDX analysis of the formed CQD@Pd-NPs has further been used to determine the elemental composition of the formed catalyst [34]. The spectrum showed the presence of Carbon (C), Oxygen (O), and Nitrogen (N) as non-metallic and Palladium (Pd) as metallic components with the atomic percentages of 26.89, 25.66, 2.20, and 45.55 % respectively in CQD@Pd-NPs (Fig. S2 a). The outcomes further justified the absence of any kind of impurities in the formed nano-catalyst (Fig. S2a). The synthesized CQD@Pd-NPs were analyzed by X-Ray diffraction analysis and the obtained peaks have been illustrated in Fig. 2a. The sharp diffraction peaks confirmed the crystalline nature of CQD@Pd-NPs [35]. The five major diffraction peaks at 2θ value of 40.27°, 46.7°, 68.33°, 82.11°, and 86.74° corresponds to (111), (200), (220), (311) and (222) planes of face centered cubic lattice of Pd. The data was found to be in good agreement with JCPDS number: 87-0638 [36]. In addition, no additional peak is detected in the spectrum which determines the purity of the sample. Further, the crystallite size was found to be 80–100 nm using Scherer formula [37].

The thermal stability of CQD@Pd-NPs was further assessed using TGA/DSC analysis in the temperature range of 30–1000 °C [38]. The curve showed that the formed nano-catalyst gets decomposed in two subsequent steps (Fig. 2b). The initial weight loss of 37.04 % is observed up to 460 °C. This loss in weight was attributed to the loss of ionic liquid molecules or dissociation of low molecular weight components immobilized over the superficial sites of CQD@Pd-NPs. The average weight loss of the nanocatalyst during the first step was found to be 1.393 mg. However, in the second step, 21.54 % of weight loss was attributed to the decomposition of residual organic functional groups from the exterior surface of CQD@Pd-NPs (Fig. 2b). The second decomposition caused a weight loss of about 0.81 mg. The DSC spectrum displayed two endothermic peaks due to the decomposition of the nanocatalyst (Fig. S2b). A sharp endothermic peak obtained at 295 °C was ascribed to the decomposition of various hydroxyl groups attached over the surface of CQD@Pd-NPs. However, the second endothermic peak at 425 °C represents the loss of organic groups which were deeply immobilized over the superficial sites of CQD@Pd-NPs. A small endothermic peak recorded at 554.6 °C was due to the thermal degradation of some of the molecules which were deeply bound to the CQD@Pd-NPs. Moreover, the apparent zeta potential results of colloidal suspension showed an absolute negative charge of –22.6 mV on CQD@Pd-NPs (Fig. S2c). The nanocatalyst was further subjected to elemental mapping for chemical composition determination and purity estimation. As evident from EDX results, the results complement the EDX studies with particles displaying maximum concentration of Palladium, Carbon, and Oxygen followed by Nitrogen (Fig. 2c). The prepared nanocatalyst was further characterized via using FESEM and HRTEM studies for exploring the shape and cross-section morphology of CQD@Pd-NPs (Fig. 2d and 2e). The formed nanocatalyst appeared to be well distributed with each average particle size ranging between 100 and 150 nm [39,40]. The particles appeared to be hexagonal with crystalline morphology. The successful immobilization of CQDs on Pd-NPs was further visualized using HRTEM studies. The image showed the presence of CQDs over the surface of Pd-NPs with a uniform particle size of 5 nm (Fig. 2e). Moreover, the information on actual hydrodynamic radii of CQDs@PdNPs was observed through Dynamic Light Scattering (DLS) measurements. The cumulant analysis of DLS results clearly supports the size of 143 nm which is greater than size estimated through XRD results.

This is due to the fact that DLS outcomes showcase the average particle size along with its hydration layer while the XRD results measures the crystallite size of particles (Fig. S3a). Further, the photoluminescence behaviour of CQD@Pd-NPs was studied via obtaining fluorescence spectra of bare CQDs and CQD@Pd-NPs. The quenching and colour changing property of CQDs thus explains the successful incorporation of CQDs onto the surface of palladium nanoparticles (Fig. S3b). The formed nanocomposite triggered the idea of developing palladium nanoparticles with the use of plant extract as reducing agent.

The catalytic activity of CQD@Pd-NPs was further assessed by performing the Suzuki-Miyaura and Mizoroki-Heck coupling reactions. Primarily, optimizations were performed by varying the nature of the base, dosage amount of nanocatalyst, reaction time, and type of solvents in both reactions for facilitate the reaction kinetics for better yield (Fig. S4). The chosen test substrate was iodobenzene and phenyl boronic acid for Suzuki-Miyaura reaction. However, the combination of styrene and iodobenzene was chosen for Mizoroki-Heck coupling reactions. On comparing the results, it was established that 2 mg CQD@Pd-NPs in the presence of K2CO3 as the base with EtOH/H2O (1:1, v-v) has exhibited a higher yield in just 20 min for Suzuki-Miyaura reaction. Moreover, in the case of Mizoroki-Heck reaction, the presence of potassium carbonate at 80 °C has displayed the highest efficacy with 5 mg of CQD@Pd-NPs in EtOH/H2O (5 mL; 1:1 v-v)) within 30 min (Fig. S4). The recyclable ability was further tested for accessing the potential scope of the prepared nanocatalyst. The simple centrifugation was followed by thoroughly washing with deionized water and absolute ethanol which was employed after each cycle to recover the nanocatalyst.

The adaptability of CQD@Pd-NPs was further checked on a diverse range of substrates including electron-deficient groups, such as nitro (–NO2), along with electron-releasing groups like methyl (–CH3) and amino (–NH2), when positioned at the para or ortho positions of aryl iodides. All the chosen groups exhibited smooth Suzuki-Miyaura coupling reactions with phenyl boronic acid as well as with 4-methyl-phenyl boronic acid in the presence of the CQD@Pd-NPs with 90–95 % yield (Fig. 3). In addition, aryl bromides and aryl chlorides also exhibited remarkable reactivity when coupled with phenyl boronic acid and resulting in the formation of desired products with good yields in presence of CQD@Pd-NPs (Fig. 3).

All the synthesized products were characterized using 1H and 13C NMR and compared with data present in literature [41]. The catalytic activity of CQD@Pd-NPs nanocatalyst was also examined in the Mizoroki-Heck coupling reaction to broaden the scope of our research. The corresponding aryl iodides, bromides, and chlorides bearing electron-donating and electron-withdrawing groups lead to the successful formation of the desired products in the presence of CQD@Pd-NPs. Moreover, experimental findings also indicated that aryl iodides exhibited outstanding yields in comparison to aryl bromides, which further surpassed the yields obtained from aryl chlorides. Furthermore, the catalytic proficiency of CQD@Pd-NPs was evident in facilitating the coupling reactions among aryl iodides and bromides with various olefins, including 4-chlorostyrene and 4-methylstyrene. It is significant to mention that 1H NMR spectral analysis supported the absolute 100 % selectivity for trans-product in all Heck olefination reactions by using this nanocatalyst.39 In the light of the aforementioned experimental finding, and theoretical computation analysis the conceivable reaction mechanism for the catalysis of Suzuki-Miyaura and Mizoroki-Heck coupling reactions by CQD@Pd-NPs is also presented in (Fig. 3 a and 3b). The results were also compared with the existing literature (Table S1) and it was established that the prepared nanocatalyst works well and supported the broad practical versatility of prepared CQD@Pd-NPs in organic transformation reactions [42,43]. The comparison has also supported that CQD@Pd-NPs exhibited good catalytic performance about both reaction yield and reaction time in both the model reactions.

The antimicrobial activity of CQD@Pd-NPs was examined against pathogenic gram-positive and gram-negative bacterial strains i.e., S. aureus and E. coli respectively by employing the inhibition zone method (Fig. 4 a and 4b). The three different concentrations (1 mg/mL, 2 mg/mL, and 3 mg/mL) of CQD@Pd-NPs were tested on the bacterial growth after the incubation of 12 h, and water was chosen as the control system during the analysis. The synthesized Pd nanocatalyst exhibited efficient antibacterial activity against both bacterial strains [44,45]. However, the quantitative zone of inhibition indicates a lower antibacterial effect on E. coli in contrast to S. aureus at an equivalent concentration of CQD@Pd-NPs (Fig. 4b). The explanation behind this mechanism could be explained by the charge and permeability of the bacterial membrane. In the case of Gram-positive bacteria, the particles stick more easily over the bacterial cell membrane due to stronger electrostatic interaction between them. After the internalization of the nanoparticles, variations in the physiological characteristics of cell like ROS generation and metabolic reactions take place. Hence, the Gram-positive bacterial cells induced the production of more ROS in comparison to gram-negative bacteria strain. The percent of damaged cells and the extent of bacterial cell distortion increased with the increasing concentration of CQD@Pd-NPs.

The phenomenon of apoptosis was further investigated by analyzing the FESEM images of bacterial cells after treatment with nanoparticles (Fig. 4). It was observed that the S. aureus and E. coli were found to retain their membrane integrity and display clear and smooth edges in the control system (Fig. 4). On the contrary, CQD@Pd-NPs treated cells indicated strong attachment of particles to the bacterial outer membrane which causes surface collapsing and perforation (Fig. 4). In particular, some of the cells showed significant morphological changes with destroyed surface and pit formation [46]. Further it was found that the bacterial disruption was more in the case of S. aureus bacterial strain due to charge interaction between the outer cell membrane of bacteria and CQD@Pd-NPs. The neutralization causes stiff attachment of CQD@Pd-NPs around the bacterial cell wall. The outcomes supported superior antibacterial activities of CQD@Pd-NPs against S. aureus (Fig. 4). The results further supported the higher penetration rate of CQD@Pd-NPs through bacterial cell walls and leading to the effluence on intracellular content and ultimate cell death. CQD@Pd-NPs demonstrated promising bactericidal efficacy against both E. coli and S. aureus, where in silico predictions exhibited favorable concordance with the bactericidal activities. Its molecular docking investigations were further conducted to acquire detailed information about the interaction of prepared CQD@Pd-NPs with targeted enzymes in the chosen bacterial system. Two pivotal enzymes involved in bacterial proliferation, namely DNA gyrase (participating in nucleic acid synthesis) and β-lactamase enzyme (participating in cell-wall synthesis) were chosen for the analysis. The docking score and interactions of prepared nanocatalyst i.e., CQD@Pd-NPs were assessed within the active pocket of the docked complexes. The binding energy i.e., quantum of energy liberated upon the union of a ligand with the active sites of a protein was assessed for the chosen system. It is worth noting that the binding energy is inversely proportional to the stability of the protein–ligand complex, with a greater negativity indicating a higher degree of stability. In the current analysis, CQDs exhibited a docked conformation that displayed hydrogen bonding interactions with Asn190(1.82Ȧ), Asp217(1.92Ȧ), Phe170(2.50Ȧ), Ala215(2.31Ȧ), Val125(2.06Ȧ), Lys126(1.83Ȧ), Glu219(2.55Ȧ) and a binding score of −8.78 kcal/mol for β-lactamase enzyme E. coli (Fig. 5 , S5 and Table S2). On the other hand, the docked conformation revealed hydrogen bonding interactions with Val125(3.30Ȧ), Lys126(2.12Ȧ), Glu219(1.90Ȧ), Leu168(2.14Ȧ), and Glu171(2.21Ȧ) with an overall binding score of −9.73 kcal/mol, in presence of CQD@Pd-NPs for β-lactamase enzyme E. coli (Fig. 5, S5 and Table S2). The hydrogen bonding interactions in case of CQDs were observed between Arg206(2.29Ȧ), Thr62(2.12Ȧ), Lys208(1.87Ȧ), Gln135(2.06Ȧ), Arg142(2.01Ȧ), and Glu131(1.93Ȧ) with a binding score of −7.64 kcal/mol for DNA gyrase E. coli (Fig. 5, S5 and Table S2). However, the bonding occurs with Arg206(1.70Ȧ), Lys208(1.80Ȧ), Gln135(2.12Ȧ), Arg142(2.14Ȧ), Glu131(1.75Ȧ), Arg168(3.12Ȧ) and has a binding score of −7.88 kcal/mol been observed in the presence of CQD@Pd-NPs for DNA gyrase E. coli (Fig. 5, S5 and Table S2).

On interpreting the results of theoretical affinity binding energies values, it becomes evident that our prepared CQD@Pd-NPs exhibit an outstanding binding score of −9.73 kcal/mol affinity towards the β-lactamase E. coli enzyme in contrast with the DNA gyrase E. coli enzyme. In a similar manner, the docking analysis of the β-lactamase S. aureus enzyme revealed that CQDs exhibited hydrogen bonding interactions with Lys322(1.73Ȧ), Asp320(2.19Ȧ), and His293(2.43Ȧ) (Fig. 6 , S6 and Table S3), resulting in an overall binding score of −9.85 kcal/mol. On the other hand, the CQD@Pd-NPs displayed a significantly higher binding score of −9.94 kcal/mol, mainly attributed to the hydrogen bonding interactions with Asp320(1.70Ȧ) and Gly271(2.06Ȧ) (Fig. 6, S6 and Table S3). Moreover, the docking predictions for DNA gyrase derived from S. aureus were additionally evaluated to examine the binding capability of CQDs (binding score: −7.56 kcal mol−1) and CQD@Pd-NPs (binding score: −7.86 kcal mol−1) towards active sites. The primary molecular associations observed CQDs were hydrogen bonding with Thr62(2.12Ȧ), Arg206(2.29Ȧ), Lys208(1.88Ȧ), Gln135(2.06Ȧ), Arg142(2.02Ȧ), Glu131(1.93 Ȧ) (Fig. 6, S6 and Table S3), while CQD@Pd-NPs engaged in hydrogen bonding with Arg142(2.10Ȧ), Glu131(1.91), Arg206(1.73Ȧ) and Gln135(2.07Ȧ) (Fig. 6, S6 and Table S3). In this case also, the β-lactamase S. aureus enzyme exhibits commendable antibacterial efficacy as opposed to the DNA gyrase S. aureus enzyme. The molecular docking investigations additionally suggest that our synthesized CQD@Pd-NPs demonstrated remarkable antibacterial properties against S. aureus as evidenced by a higher binding energy value (-9.94 kcal/mol).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Reusability and recovery efficiency of CQD@Pd-NPs
ce:section.ce:para: A comprehensive examination was performed to assess the reusability and recoverability of CQD@Pd-NPs on designated test substrates in both the model reaction under optimized reaction condition. The preliminary investigations were conducted in the presence of CQD@Pd-NPs for both Suzuki-Miyaura and Mizoroki-Heck coupling reactions. The chosen experimental substrates comprised iodobenzene and phenyl boronic acid for the Suzuki-Miyaura reaction. Conversely, the styrene and iodobenzene was selected for Mizoroki-Heck coupling reactions for recovery study like presented in (Fig. 3a-b, entry 1). Following the completion of the catalytic experiments, the CQD@Pd-NPs nanocatalyst utilized was extracted from the reaction mixture by using a centrifugation process and underwent a water rinse, desiccation, and consequent utilization in successive catalytic iterations. The reusability and recoverability tests demonstrated that the prepared nanocatalyst displayed consistent activity over seven successive cycles, leading to yields of 95 % and 96 % for the Suzuki-Miyaura and Mizoroki-Heck coupling reactions, respectively (Fig. 7 ). The CQD@Pd-NPs demonstrates minimal catalytic activity degradation even after seven cycles, showcasing the exceptional stability of prepared nanocatalyst.

In summary, a novel CQD@Pd-NPs nanocatalyst has been designed using Aegle marmelos fruit-derived CQDs. The combination of biomass-derived CQDs and Pd-NPs act as an efficient and stable nanocatalysts for Suzuki-Miyaura and Mizoroki-Heck coupling reactions. Under optimized reaction conditions, the CQD@Pd-NPs catalyst demonstrates exceptional efficiency in producing the corresponding products with favorable yields. A diverse array of Suzuki and Heck coupled products were successfully isolated utilizing moderate reaction conditions, resulting in reduced reaction time and the use of water as an environmentally friendly medium without the need for any further reagents. The nanocatalyst recycled over seven successive runs and hence exhibits promising results in contrast to conventional Pd-NPs. The shorter reaction time, reduced catalyst amount, and improved catalytic activity corresponded to the presence of various functional groups on their surface which also imparted superior antibacterial response towards bacterial strains. Additionally, the antibacterial response of CQD@Pd-NPs towards Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacterial strains was proved with molecular docking studies. The nanocatalyst displayed facile separation from the reaction system and showcased efficient recyclability with minimal activity loss and supports an effective potential over existing commercial catalysts and holds immense future prospects in industrial applications. Incessant achievements in developing advanced nanocatalyst supports CQD@Pd-NPs as an ideal system for industrial application over the commercial nanoparticles. This greener approach could possibly result in economical automation system.
